From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Canadian actress (born 1972)

For the Indian American actress and fashion designer known professionally as LisaRaye, see [LisaRaye McCoy](/wiki/LisaRaye_McCoy "LisaRaye McCoy").

**Lisa Rani Ray** (Bengali pronunciation: [[lisa rae̯]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Bengali "Help:IPA/Bengali"); born 4 April 1972)[[1]](#cite_note-Age-1) is a Canadian actress. She began her modelling career in India in the early 1990s, appearing for leading Indian brands like [Bombay Dyeing](/wiki/Bombay_Dyeing "Bombay Dyeing") and [Lakmé](/wiki/Lakm%C3%A9_Cosmetics "Lakmé Cosmetics"). She made her acting debut in 1994 in the film *[Hanste Khelte](/wiki/Hanste_Khelte_(1994_film) "Hanste Khelte (1994 film)").* Through her acting career, Ray has demonstrated a penchant for issue-oriented portrayals, most notably in the 2005 Oscar-nominated Canadian film *[Water](/wiki/Water_(2005_film) "Water (2005 film)")* and the award-winning South African feature *[The World Unseen](/wiki/The_World_Unseen "The World Unseen")*, described by a reviewer as "one of the best-conceived queer films of the past year."[[2]](#cite_note-2)

In 2009, Ray was diagnosed with [multiple myeloma](/wiki/Multiple_myeloma "Multiple myeloma"), an incurable form of blood cancer.[[3]](#cite_note-3) She began writing *The Yellow Diaries,* a blog about her experiences of having cancer.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Her writing and columns have since regularly appeared in multiple major publications.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) Ray remains an active advocate of [stem-cell therapy](/wiki/Stem-cell_therapy "Stem-cell therapy")[[7]](#cite_note-7) and has participated in several successful fundraisers and cancer awareness campaigns.

In 2011, Ray began hosting a popular travel show on [Discovery Channel India](/wiki/Discovery_Channel_(India) "Discovery Channel (India)")[[8]](#cite_note-8) alongside appearing as host and judge in [Food Network](/wiki/Food_Network_(Canadian_TV_channel) "Food Network (Canadian TV channel)")'s highest rated show, *[Top Chef Canada](/wiki/Top_Chef_Canada "Top Chef Canada")*.[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9)

In 2016, Ray opened an [Instagram](/wiki/Instagram "Instagram") account dedicated to poetry.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lisa_Ray&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Ray was born in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto") to an [Indian Bengali Hindu](/wiki/Bengali_Hindus "Bengali Hindus") father and a [Polish](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people") [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") mother[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) and grew up in the suburb of [Etobicoke](/wiki/Etobicoke "Etobicoke"). She spoke Polish with her maternal grandmother and watched movies of [Federico Fellini](/wiki/Federico_Fellini "Federico Fellini") and [Satyajit Ray](/wiki/Satyajit_Ray "Satyajit Ray") with her [cinephile](/wiki/Cinephile "Cinephile") dad.[[14]](#cite_note-Chatelaine-14) During her childhood she spent some time in [Calcutta](/wiki/Kolkata "Kolkata").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

She excelled academically, doing five years of high school in four, while attending three different high schools: [Etobicoke Collegiate Institute](/wiki/Etobicoke_Collegiate_Institute "Etobicoke Collegiate Institute"), [Richview Collegiate Institute](/wiki/Richview_Collegiate_Institute "Richview Collegiate Institute") and [Silverthorn Collegiate Institute](/wiki/Silverthorn_Collegiate_Institute "Silverthorn Collegiate Institute").[[16]](#cite_note-PinchSpice-16)

## Modelling, film and television career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lisa_Ray&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Modelling, film and television career")]

### 1991–2000

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lisa_Ray&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1991–2000")]

Ray's modelling career began when she was "discovered" during a family vacation in India while still in her teens. An advertisement for Bombay Dyeing where she appeared in a black swimsuit opposite [Karan Kapoor](/wiki/Karan_Kapoor "Karan Kapoor") earned Ray her first taste of public attention.[[17]](#cite_note-RayHope-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-MyDen-20) A subsequent meeting with Maureen Wadia, editor of Indian fashion magazine *[Gladrags](/wiki/Gladrags "Gladrags")*, resulted in an iconic swimsuit cover that catapulted Ray to nationwide fame in India. "Most of my most fulfilling professional moments came to me via serendipity", Ray later wrote.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

By the time the Gladrags cover broke, Ray was back in Toronto ready to begin university. Her plans were thwarted after a tragic auto accident that would consign her mother to a wheelchair for the rest of her life.[[22]](#cite_note-:1-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23) Ray returned to India and went on to become one of the country's first [supermodels](/wiki/Supermodel "Supermodel"), and the face of Lakmé and Bombay Dyeing.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Ray would later acknowledge this coinciding of professional triumph and personal tragedy as a recurring theme in her life. "I have come to recognise that every major turning point in my life is preceded by pain", she wrote in Femina in 2016.[[22]](#cite_note-:1-22)

As one of India's most successful cover models, Ray would subsequently lend her face to iconic global brands such as [L’Oréal](/wiki/L%27Or%C3%A9al "L'Oréal"), [MasterCard](/wiki/Mastercard "Mastercard"), [De Beers](/wiki/De_Beers "De Beers") and [Rado](/wiki/Rado_(watchmaker) "Rado (watchmaker)").[[25]](#cite_note-25) A *[Times of India](/wiki/Times_of_India "Times of India")* poll named her the "ninth most beautiful woman of the millennium," the only model in the top ten.[[16]](#cite_note-PinchSpice-16) She co-anchored the TV show Star Biz on [Star Movies](/wiki/Star_Movies "Star Movies")[[26]](#cite_note-26) and appeared in a music video for *[Afreen Afreen](/wiki/Afreen_Afreen "Afreen Afreen")*, a [ghazal](/wiki/Ghazal "Ghazal") written by [Javed Akhtar](/wiki/Javed_Akhtar "Javed Akhtar"), and composed and performed by [Nusrat Fateh Ali Khan](/wiki/Nusrat_Fateh_Ali_Khan "Nusrat Fateh Ali Khan").

### 2001–2009

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lisa_Ray&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2001–2009")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Lisa_Ray_on_The_Hour_cropped.jpg/250px-Lisa_Ray_on_The_Hour_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lisa_Ray_on_The_Hour_cropped.jpg)

Lisa Ray being interviewed on *[The Hour](/wiki/George_Stroumboulopoulos_Tonight "George Stroumboulopoulos Tonight")*

After turning down a number of roles,[[27]](#cite_note-27) Ray made her Indian Film Industry debut in 2001 with the Hindi film *[Kasoor](/wiki/Kasoor "Kasoor"),* opposite [Aftab Shivdasani](/wiki/Aftab_Shivdasani "Aftab Shivdasani"),[[17]](#cite_note-RayHope-17) in which her voice was dubbed by [Divya Dutta](/wiki/Divya_Dutta "Divya Dutta"), because she could not speak [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi").[[28]](#cite_note-LoveIt-28) In spite of that, her performance received positive reviews.[[29]](#cite_note-29) Her work in the film also caught the eye of [Deepa Mehta](/wiki/Deepa_Mehta "Deepa Mehta"), who cast her in the romantic Indian-Canadian romp *[Bollywood/Hollywood](/wiki/Bollywood/Hollywood "Bollywood/Hollywood")* in 2002.[[14]](#cite_note-Chatelaine-14)

Realizing that acting was something that she wanted to pursue more seriously, Ray moved to London to concentrate on a career in the performing arts. While there, Ray studied at the Central School of Speech and Drama, the London Centre for Theatre Studies, the Desmond Jones school of Physical Theatre, and BADA. She graduated from the Academy of Live and Recorded Arts (ALRA), in 2004, with a post-graduate degree in acting.[[30]](#cite_note-Lisa_Ray-30) While studying, Ray made a conscious effort to not accept any film offers until she had graduated. However, while still at ALRA, she received another call from Deepa Mehta, who made her an offer she simply could not refuse—the lead role of Kalyani in Mehta's much-anticipated, and eventually highly controversial,[[31]](#cite_note-31) feature, *[Water](/wiki/Water_(2005_film) "Water (2005 film)")*.[[30]](#cite_note-Lisa_Ray-30) In the film, Lisa spoke her lines in Hindi, although her voice was dubbed in the final cut.[[28]](#cite_note-LoveIt-28) Water released in 2005 to both national and global critical acclaim, with the venerable [Roger Ebert](/wiki/Roger_Ebert "Roger Ebert") describing it as "lovely in the way [Satyajit Ray](/wiki/Satyajit_Ray "Satyajit Ray")'s films are lovely",[[32]](#cite_note-32)

Ray has since worked in productions from Canada, Europe, and the United States. Past roles include a farm girl in *[All Hat](/wiki/All_Hat "All Hat")*, a school teacher in *[A Stone's Throw](/wiki/A_Stone%27s_Throw "A Stone's Throw"),* and a housewife in 1950s-apartheid South Africa in *[The World Unseen](/wiki/The_World_Unseen "The World Unseen").* In 2008 Ray starred, alongside [Sheetal Sheth](/wiki/Sheetal_Sheth "Sheetal Sheth") and [Amber Rose Revah](/wiki/Amber_Rose_Revah "Amber Rose Revah"), in the [Shamim Sarif](/wiki/Shamim_Sarif "Shamim Sarif") directed British romantic comedy *[I Can't Think Straight](/wiki/I_Can%27t_Think_Straight "I Can't Think Straight")* that went on to win awards in [queer film festivals](/wiki/List_of_LGBT_film_festivals "List of LGBT film festivals") worldwide, including Dallas OUT TAKES, Miami Gay and Lesbian Film Festival, and [Tampa International Gay and Lesbian Film festival](/wiki/Tampa_International_Gay_and_Lesbian_Film_Festival "Tampa International Gay and Lesbian Film Festival").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

After graduation, Ray based herself out of Milan, Paris, and New York from 2004 to 2008, returning to Toronto upon her mother's death in late 2008.[[30]](#cite_note-Lisa_Ray-30) In 2007, Ray completed filming for *[Kill Kill Faster Faster](/wiki/Kill_Kill_Faster_Faster "Kill Kill Faster Faster")*, which is a contemporary film noir inspired by the critically acclaimed novel of the same name, by [Joel Rose](/wiki/Joel_Rose "Joel Rose"). She guest-starred in a 2009 episode of the [USA Network](/wiki/USA_Network "USA Network") series *[Psych](/wiki/Psych "Psych")*,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and appeared in [Woody Harrelson](/wiki/Woody_Harrelson "Woody Harrelson") starrer Canadian-American superhero film *[Defendor](/wiki/Defendor "Defendor")*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Also in 2009, she starred in the Deepa and Dilip Mehta comedy *[Cooking with Stella](/wiki/Cooking_with_Stella "Cooking with Stella")*.

Appearing at the 2009 *[Toronto International Film Festival](/wiki/Toronto_International_Film_Festival "Toronto International Film Festival")* in support of the film, Ray revealed that she had been diagnosed with multiple myeloma.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### 2010s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lisa_Ray&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2010s")]

On [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas") in 2009, Ray received a [stem cell transplant](/wiki/Stem_cell_transplant "Stem cell transplant") to treat her rare cancer. In April 2010, she announced she was cancer-free due to the transplant. She gave a candid interview on her personal cancer trauma and surviving it, appearing on the cover of the 2010 anniversary issue of the Indian men's luxury magazine *The Man*.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

2010 saw Ray in UniGlobe Entertainment's cancer docu-drama titled *[1 a Minute](/wiki/1_a_Minute "1 a Minute")*.[[35]](#cite_note-MCG-35) The documentary was made by [Namrata Singh Gujral](/wiki/Namrata_Singh_Gujral "Namrata Singh Gujral") and featured cancer survivors [Olivia Newton-John](/wiki/Olivia_Newton-John "Olivia Newton-John"), [Diahann Carroll](/wiki/Diahann_Carroll "Diahann Carroll"), [Melissa Etheridge](/wiki/Melissa_Etheridge "Melissa Etheridge"), [Mumtaz](/wiki/Mumtaz_(Indian_actress) "Mumtaz (Indian actress)"), [Bárbara Mori](/wiki/B%C3%A1rbara_Mori "Bárbara Mori") and [Jaclyn Smith](/wiki/Jaclyn_Smith "Jaclyn Smith"), as well as [William Baldwin](/wiki/William_Baldwin "William Baldwin"), [Daniel Baldwin](/wiki/Daniel_Baldwin "Daniel Baldwin"), and [Priya Dutt](/wiki/Priya_Dutt "Priya Dutt"), whose lives have been touched by cancer. The feature was narrated by [Kelly McGillis](/wiki/Kelly_McGillis "Kelly McGillis").

On 5 July 2010, Ray hosted an informal lunch for [Queen Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") and [Prince Philip](/wiki/Prince_Philip,_Duke_of_Edinburgh "Prince Philip, Duke of Edinburgh") during their visit to Toronto.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

In 2011, Ray acted in the stage play *Taj*, opposite [Kabir Bedi](/wiki/Kabir_Bedi "Kabir Bedi") at [Luminato Festival, Toronto's International Festival of Arts and Ideas](/wiki/Luminato "Luminato").[[37]](#cite_note-Taj:_more_than_a_love_story-37) Written by Canadian playwright [John Murrell](/wiki/John_Murrell_(playwright) "John Murrell (playwright)"), *Taj* combined poetry, music, theatre, and the Indian classical dance form [kathak](/wiki/Kathak "Kathak").[[38]](#cite_note-38) The production was described by *[The Globe and Mail](/wiki/The_Globe_and_Mail "The Globe and Mail")* critic Deirdre Kelly "as luminous and poignant as the building that inspired it".[[39]](#cite_note-39)

In the same year, Ray appeared in Craig Goodwill's award-winning[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] fantasy drama film *[Patch Town](/wiki/Patch_Town_(2014_film) "Patch Town (2014 film)")*, and a [season 4](/wiki/List_of_Murdoch_Mysteries_episodes#Season_4_(2011) "List of Murdoch Mysteries episodes") episode of Canadian Television period crime drama series *[Murdoch Mysteries](/wiki/Murdoch_Mysteries "Murdoch Mysteries")*, titled "The Black Hand".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

She presented the 2011 [IIFA Awards](/wiki/IIFA_Awards "IIFA Awards")[[40]](#cite_note-40) in Toronto, and was a co-presenter the 2011 [Giller Prize](/wiki/Giller_Prize "Giller Prize") along with singer-songwriter [Nelly Furtado](/wiki/Nelly_Furtado "Nelly Furtado") and [roots rock](/wiki/Roots_rock "Roots rock") guitarist [Robbie Robertson](/wiki/Robbie_Robertson "Robbie Robertson").[[41]](#cite_note-41)

Also in 2011, Ray appeared as host and travel guide on *Oh My Gold*, a five part series on [Discovery Travel & Living](/wiki/Discovery_Travel_%26_Living "Discovery Travel & Living"), now [TLC India](/wiki/TLC_(Indian_TV_channel) "TLC (Indian TV channel)").

In 2012, Ray began hosting season two of *[Top Chef Canada](/wiki/Top_Chef_Canada "Top Chef Canada")*, [Food Network Canada](/wiki/Flavour_Network "Flavour Network")'s highest rated series. The season premiered in March 2012.[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9)

Speaking to [*The Telegraph*](/wiki/The_Telegraph_(Kolkata) "The Telegraph (Kolkata)") in 2012, Ray remarked, "I think every film that I’ve done so far has been a turning point because I experimented with each one and grew professionally. The movies I chose, dealt with a lot of thought-provoking subjects.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

In 2016, Ray starred in [Ram Gopal Varma](/wiki/Ram_Gopal_Varma "Ram Gopal Varma")'s [*Veerappan*](/wiki/Veerappan_(2016_film) "Veerappan (2016 film)").[[43]](#cite_note-43) She also played a crucial supporting role in 2017’s [*Dobaara*](/wiki/Dobaara:_See_Your_Evil "Dobaara: See Your Evil"), the official Bollywood adaptation of supernatural horror film *[Oculus](/wiki/Oculus_(film) "Oculus (film)")*.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

Since 2019, Ray has appeared as part of an ensemble cast in the [Amazon Prime Video](/wiki/Amazon_Prime_Video "Amazon Prime Video") web series *[Four More Shots Please!](/wiki/Four_More_Shots_Please! "Four More Shots Please!")*.[[45]](#cite_note-45) After a successful first season aired in over 200 countries and regions, the series returned for a second season in 2020 and a third season in 2022.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lisa_Ray&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Personal life")]

On 23 June 2009, Ray was diagnosed with [multiple myeloma](/wiki/Multiple_myeloma "Multiple myeloma"), a cancer of the white blood cells known as plasma cells, which produce antibodies. It is a rare disease.[[28]](#cite_note-LoveIt-28)[[47]](#cite_note-47) In April 2010, she announced that she was cancer-free, after an [autologous stem cell transplant](/wiki/Autologous_stem_cell_transplant "Autologous stem cell transplant") using her own stem cells.[[48]](#cite_note-free-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49) As multiple myeloma is an incurable disease, Ray is not completely cured of the disease.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

In February 2012, Ray announced her engagement to management consultant Jason Dehni.[[51]](#cite_note-51) On 20 October 2012, Ray and Dehni (then a banking executive) were married in [California](/wiki/California "California")'s [Napa Valley](/wiki/Napa_Valley "Napa Valley").[[52]](#cite_note-52)

In September 2018, Ray announced that she and her husband became parents to twin daughters via [surrogacy](/wiki/Surrogacy "Surrogacy"), in June 2018.[[53]](#cite_note-53)
